original_abstract: Direct functionalization of methane selectively to value-added chemicals is still one of the main challenges in modern science. Acetic acid is an important industrial chemical produced nowadays by expensive and environmentally unfriendly carbonylation of methanol using homogeneous catalysts. Here, we report a new photocatalytic reaction route to synthesize acetic acid from CH4 and CO at room temperature using water as the sole external oxygen source. The optimized photocatalyst consists of a TiO2 support and ammonium phosphotungstic polyoxometalate (NPW) clusters anchored with isolated Pt single atoms (Pt1). It enables a stable synthesis of 5.7 mmol·L-1 acetic acid solution in 60 h with the selectivity over 90% and 66% to acetic acid on liquid-phase and carbon basis, respectively, with the production of 99 mol of acetic acid per mol of Pt. Combined isotopic and in situ spectroscopy investigation suggests that synthesis of acetic acid proceeds via a photocatalytic oxidative carbonylation of methane over the Pt1 sites, with the methane activation facilitated by water-derived hydroxyl radicals.
humanized_abstract: Despite much effort, direct catalytic conversion of methane to chemical commodities is highly inefficient and remains a grand challenge. The current production of acetic acid using transition metal homogeneous catalysts based on the high energy input of the catalytic carbonylation of methanol process is energetically inefficient and highly unsustainable. We have developed a new, room temperature water-mediated, photo-activation catalyst that can directly catalyse the conversion of CH4 and CO2 to give a solution of high concentration of acetic acid under illumination. A TiO2 supported ammonium phosphotungstic polyoxometalate anchored with isolated Pt single atoms (Pt1@NPW/TiO2) shows the highest yield of acetic acid with a solution concentration as high as 5.7 mmol·L-1 in 60 h with selectivity > 90% and 66% on liquid and carbon basis, respectively. The catalyst gives an unparalleled productivity of 99 acetic acid moles per mole of Pt. Using combined isotopic labelling experiments and in situ bulk and surface 13C NMR, we show that the aqueous photo-activation of the high performance catalyst yields acetic acid via a catalytic oxidative carbonylation of methane process centred about the Pt1 sites, in which the activation of methane is highly enhanced in the presence of hydroxyl species derived from water.
